narrative: As a white male applicant with a solid undergraduate GPA of 3.70 and an LSAT score of 37, you brought some strong academic credentials to your law school application, along with your upper-middle-class family background and plans to attend full-time. However, the admissions model, which predicts the likelihood of passing the bar exam on the first try, estimated your probability of failure at 45.9%—high enough to trigger a rejection, as the school prioritizes applicants predicted to succeed right away. The key factor driving this decision was your Year 3 school decile ranking of 1, which stood out well below the typical student's average of around 5.5 and had the strongest pull toward the failure prediction by signaling weaker performance in later undergraduate years. This low ranking outweighed your other strengths, as it was the most influential element in the model's assessment for you specifically. On a positive note, your undergraduate GPA was a major counterbalance, ranking above the average of 3.21 and significantly pushing the prediction toward expecting you to pass the bar. Similarly, your LSAT score, slightly above the typical 36.7, provided another helpful boost in the model's eyes, nudging it away from failure. Compared to most students in the dataset, where full-time enrollment is the norm at over 92%, your choice there didn't stand out but fit the successful profile. Overall, while your early Year 1 decile of 1 was concerning, it was the drop-off reflected in Year 3 that tipped the scales most heavily against you relative to peers who maintain higher rankings throughout. This isn't a judgment on your potential as a white male from an upper-middle-income family—many in similar situations succeed with targeted improvements. To move forward, consider retaking courses or programs to boost your later-year decile rankings, perhaps through a post-baccalaureate or targeted GPA enhancement, and reapply after demonstrating that upward trend. You could also seek feedback from admissions counselors on bolstering your narrative around these metrics. With your existing GPA and LSAT as anchors, addressing that Year 3 performance could flip the model's outlook in your favor next time. Keep pushing—you've got a strong foundation to build on.
